coredata.dc:title: In-situ growth of vertical graphene on titanium by PECVD for rapid sterilization under near-infrared light
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xianming
Qiu, Jiajun
Tan, Ji
Zhang, Dongdong
Wu, Ling
Qiao, Yuqin
Wang, Guocheng
Wu, Jun
Yeung, Kelvin W.K.
Liu, Xuanyong
coredata.dc:description: Alternative antimicrobials are urgently needed due to the antibiotic resistance. Herein, vertical graphene has been in-situ prepared on medical titanium (VG@Ti) by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition for rapid sterilization under near-infrared light. The physicochemical properties, photothermal effect, antibacterial activity, and biocompatibility of VG@Ti were systematically investigated. The results indicated that the thickness of vertical graphene film increased with the augmentation of reaction temperature. Graphene films showed a vertical distribution on medical titanium, which improved the corrosion resistance, hydrophobicity as well as antibacterial ability against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) under near-infrared light. Inhibition rates of 93.3% and 99.1% were achieved for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, when VG@Ti was exposed to near-infrared light (808 nm) with 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. Moreover, vertical graphene on titanium surface exhibit no obvious cytotoxicity to osteoblasts cells. The in vivo results confirmed that vertical graphene with near-infrared light could kill bacteria efficiently and showed no obvious toxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium
Vertical graphene
Plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition
Rapid sterilization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of vertical graphene on titanium surface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) Raman spectra of various samples; (b) XRD patterns of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750; (c) Raman mapping of I
D
/I
G ratio from VG@Ti-650; (d) Raman mapping of I
D
/I
G ratio from VG@Ti-750; (e) Raman mapping of I
2D
/I
G ratio from VG@Ti-650; (f) Raman mapping of I
2D
/I
G ratio from VG@Ti-750; (g) SEM surface morphologies of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750; (h) Cross-section morphologies of VG@Ti-650, VG@Ti-750, and (i) corresponding thickness of vertical graphene films.

Water contact angles (a), potentiodynamic polarization curves (b), zeta potentials (c), and UV–vis–NIR spectra (d) of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750; Changes of surface temperature of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 in water under NIR irradiation (e) and Corresponding images showing the temperature distribution (f); Changes of surface temperature of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 in air under NIR irradiation (g); Temperature variations of VG@Ti-750 along with the on and off of NIR light (h).

SEM morphological of E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b) on Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750. Photographs of cultivated E. coli and S. aureus on agar plates (c) and corresponding inhibition rates of E. coli and S. aureus with NIR (d). Fluorescent images of live/dead cell staining of E. coli in control group (e) and NIR group (f); Fluorescent images of live/dead cell staining of S. aureus in control group (g) and NIR group (h). All the data are expressed as means ± SD (n = 3). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001).

(a) Fluorescent images of F-actin stained with FITC (green) and the nucleus stained with DAPI (blue) of MC3T3-E1 cells cultured on various sample surfaces for 1, 4, and 24 h; (b) Fluorescent images of live/dead cell staining of MC3T3-E1 cells cultured on various sample surfaces for 4 days; (c) Cell proliferation of MC3T3-E1 cells cultured on various samples; (d) SEM morphologies of MC3T3-E1 cultured on various sample surfaces for 1, 4, and 7 days at high and low magnification. All the data are expressed as means ± SD (n = 4). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001).

(a) The schematic diagram of phototherapy through NIR. (b) Changes of surface temperature of different samples in vivo under NIR irradiation, and (c) Corresponding images showing the temperature distribution. (d) Photographs of cultivated S. aureus on agar plate and (e) corresponding photothermal inhibition rates of S. aureus. (f) The H&E staining of tissue near the implant at 1 day. All the data are expressed as means ± SD (n = 3). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001).

head.ce:title: In-situ growth of vertical graphene on titanium by PECVD for rapid sterilization under near-infrared light

Shanghai Institute of Ceramics
Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alternative antimicrobials are urgently needed due to the antibiotic resistance. Herein, vertical graphene has been in-situ prepared on medical titanium (VG@Ti) by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition for rapid sterilization under near-infrared light. The physicochemical properties, photothermal effect, antibacterial activity, and biocompatibility of VG@Ti were systematically investigated. The results indicated that the thickness of vertical graphene film increased with the augmentation of reaction temperature. Graphene films showed a vertical distribution on medical titanium, which improved the corrosion resistance, hydrophobicity as well as antibacterial ability against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) under near-infrared light. Inhibition rates of 93.3% and 99.1% were achieved for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, when VG@Ti was exposed to near-infrared light (808 nm) with 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. Moreover, vertical graphene on titanium surface exhibit no obvious cytotoxicity to osteoblasts cells. The in vivo results confirmed that vertical graphene with near-infrared light could kill bacteria efficiently and showed no obvious toxicity.

Vertical graphene is incorporated on titanium by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition. Vertical graphene has excellent photothermal properties, which can achieve rapid sterilization under near-infrared light.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection is the main medical complication of implants [1–3]. To address this problem, surface modifications are needed to endow the implants with excellent antibacterial properties. Typical bactericidal surfaces are mainly based on biocidal agents including metal nanoparticles [4–6], antimicrobial enzymes [7], antimicrobial peptides [8], synthetic antibacterial polymers [3], and antibiotics [9,10]. However, metal nanoparticles including Ag [4,11], Cu [12], and ZnO [13] can release toxic substances, which usually have serious side effects on host cells. Natural antibacterial peptides and enzymes are generally limited because of high susceptibility to proteolysis and low stability [8]. Synthetic antibacterial polymers have the disadvantages of high manufacturing cost, synthetic complexity, stability, and toxicity at high doses [14,15]. Traditional antibiotics are gradually losing their effectiveness as a result of the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria [16]. Hence, it is urgently demanded to develop novel and effective antibacterial strategies.

Recently, phototherapy methods have been widely noticed for their nondestructive feature, feasibility, and high spatiotemporal precision, including photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [17–23]. PTT mainly depends on the significant hyperthermia produced by the photothermal agents to kill bacteria without bacterial resistance when the agents are under near-infrared (NIR) light [18,24,25]. Wu et al. reported that PTT was able to scavenge the existing biofilms and prevent the formation of new biofilms and kill planktonic bacteria [26]. Therefore, many prominent photothermal materials [27], including gold nanoparticles [28], black phosphorus [5,29], carbon nanotubes [30], transition metal oxide/sulfide nanoparticles [31–33], and graphene materials (GMs) [34–36] have been actively investigated and utilized for photothermal antibacterial application. Among them, GMs are particularly attractive in terms of their tunable structure, high photothermal conversion potency, and good biocompatibility [37–39]. Dai et al. developed biocompatible, nanosized, reduced graphene oxide sheets with high NIR absorbance for potential PTT [40]. Wang et al. reported that graphene oxide developed by chemical assembly technique on micro-grooved Ti–6Al–4V implants improved surface wettability and bone-like apatite formation, and promoted cell adhesion, proliferation, and osteogenic differentiation [41]. Jia and co-workers developed graphene-based self-assemblies on orthopedic titanium, and selectively regulated bacterial activities and osteoblastic functions [42]. Qiu et al. reported that graphene oxide prepared by cathodal electrophoretic deposition on the titanium surfaces exhibited excellent osteogenic differentiation and antibacterial effects [43]. However, graphene films prepared by the above methods have a common disadvantage that the bonding strength between graphene film and substrate is weak.

Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is commonly used to fabricate graphene films on metal substrates which have high bonding strength between graphene films and substrates. However, process parameters for growing graphene films using CVD technique usually need high temperature [44], which is not suitable for surface modification of titanium implant because of relatively low phase transition temperature of titanium. Plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) has the advantages of a low reaction temperature which can realize in-situ preparation of graphene film on medical titanium surface with high bonding strength. Based on this, in the present work, graphene films were in-situ prepared on medical titanium surface by PECVD. Photothermal property, antibacterial activity, and biocompatibility of graphene films in vitro were systematically studied. Photothermal antibacterial activity of vertical graphene in vivo was assessed by Sprague-Dawley rat model of subcutaneous implantation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of vertical graphene on titanium surface
ce:section.ce:para: Commercial pure titanium (CP–Ti) plates (10 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm) were used as the growth substrate. First, these CP-Ti plates were treated with mixed aqueous solution (HNO3: HF = 4:1) to remove the oxide layer and acquire homogenous surfaces, and followed by ultrasonically washed in ethyl alcohol and ultrapure water. Accordingly, these pre-treated titanium plates were marked as Ti. PECVD was used to grow vertical graphene on titanium in-situ. As shown in Fig. S1, growth of vertical graphene on titanium substrate contained four steps: (1) Heating the titanium substrates to the required temperatures (600 °C, 650 °C, 700 °C, 750 °C, and 800 °C) with heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 under the flowing Ar (80 sccm) and H2 (5 sccm) atmosphere. (2) Keeping the desired temperature for 10 min (3) Starting up the plasma emitter (100 W) and exposing the titanium substrates to plasma atmosphere at target temperature for 60 min under the flowing gases of CH4 (2 sccm), H2 (5 sccm), and Ar (80 sccm). (4) Cooling the titanium substrates to room temperature naturally in vacuum. The obtained samples were marked as VG@Ti-600, VG@Ti-650, VG@Ti-700, VG@Ti-750, and VG@Ti-800. The preparation process is showed in Fig. S2.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Magellan 400, FEI, USA) were applied to investigate the surface morphology and thickness of vertical graphene films. The Raman spectra of various samples were measured from 1200 to 2800 cm−1 by a Raman microscope system (LabRAM, Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) using an Ar-ion laser with excitation wavelength at 514 nm. The laser intensity was adjusted to 30 mW and accumulation duration was 20 s. The phase compositions were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) with the range of scanning angle from 10° to 80°. Ultraviolet–visible near-infrared spectrometer (Lambda 750, PerkinElmer, UK) was used to study the ultraviolet–visible near-infrared absorption spectra of the samples.

Contact angle test was applied to investigate the surface wettability of various samples. To be specific, 2 μL of ultrapure water was dropped onto the sample surfaces and photographs were captured by the built-in camera. The contact angle was measured by the contact angle instrument (SL200B, Solon, China).

Streaming potential results of various samples were determined using a surpass electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Parr, Austria). Briefly, two samples (20 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm) were fastened on the sample holders, and the gap of samples was adjusted to 100 ± 5 μm. KCl solution (1 mM) was used as the testing medium, and NaOH (0.05 M) and HCl (0.05 M) were used to regulate the pH range. The zeta potentials were calculated based on the Helmholtz-Smoluchowski equation [45].

Samples with 1 mL of ultrapure water were introduced to a 24-well plate, and exposed to 808 nm laser (0.8 W/cm2) for 10 min. Infrared thermal imager (FLIR A325sc, USA) was used to recorded changes in temperature of different samples. Changes in temperature of different samples exposed to air without water under NIR (0.5 W/cm2) were also measured. Furthermore, the thermostability of VG@Ti-750 was tested by six heating and cooling cycles.

Escherichia coli (Migula) Castellani and Chalmers (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus subsp. aureus Rosenbach (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) were chosen as the typical bacteria to assess the antibacterial ability of various samples. Luria-Bertani and Nutrient Broth No. 2 media were used to culture E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The various samples were placed in a 24-well plate after sterilizing by 75 vol% alcohol and dried. 500 μL of bacteria solution with the density of 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL was introduced to each well. After culturing for 12 h at 37 °C, the samples with bacteria were exposed to 808 nm laser for 10 min at a power of 0.8 W/cm2 in experimental group (NIR group) and no laser irradiation was given to the control group. For live/dead bacteria staining, live/dead® baclight™ bacterial viability kits (Invitrogen, USA) were used. In detail, samples with bacteria were washed twice with 0.9 wt % NaCl solution and then incubated with 500 μL of dye at room temperature in the dark for 15 min. Finally, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Leica SP8, Germany) was used to observe samples with bacteria and corresponding fluorescence images were taken.

To observe the SEM morphology of bacteria on various samples, bacteria were fixed by 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution, and dehydrated with gradient alcohol and hexamethyldisilazane ethanol solution. At last, surface morphologies of bacteria were observed using SEM (S–3400 N, HITACHI, Japan).

To assess the antibacterial rate, bacteria on various samples were detached and collected. Then, the collected bacteria suspensions were diluted to 10-fold, 100-fold, and 1000-fold, and 100 μL of the 1000-fold diluted solution was introduced on agar culture plates and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. At last, bacterial colonies on the agar culture plates were counted and the inhibition rates were calculated according to the equation: inhibition rate = (Nc-Ne)/Nc × 100%, where Nc and Ne are number of bacteria colonies in control group and experimental group (NIR group), respectively.

The MC3T3-E1 Cells (Cells Resource Center, Shanghai Institute of Biological Science, China) were applied to assess the biocompatibility of various samples. The cells were cultured in α-minimum essential medium (α-MEM) with 1% antimicrobial of penicillin/streptomycin mixture (P/S, Hyclone, USA) and 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Hyclone, USA) and at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 thermostat.

To assess the initial cell adhesion and spreading, the MC3T3-E1 cells were seeded on the samples with a density of 5 × 104 cells/well. After cultivation for 1, 4, and 24 h, the cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) and permeabilized with Triton X-100. Subsequently, FITC-Phalloidin and DAPI were used to stain F-actin and nuclei, respectively. Finally, cells were observed under CLSM (Leica SP8, Germany).

To evaluate the cell proliferation, MC3T3-E1 cell with a density of 2 × 104 cells/well were seeded on the samples and cultured for 1, 4, and 7 days. At each time point, cell viability was detected via cell counting kit-8(CCK-8, Beyotime, China). To observe the cell SEM morphology on the samples, the cells were fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution for 12 h and then dehydrated in grade ethanol and hexamethyldisilazane ethanol mixture solution. Finally, the cell morphologies were observed by a SEM (S–3400 N, HITACHI, Japan).

For live/dead cell staining, MC3T3-E1 cells at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well were cultured on the samples and cultured for 4 days. Then, the samples with cells were placed into a new 24-well plate and cleaned twice with PBS. Next, a live/dead cell staining kit (BioVision, USA) was used to stain the cells at 37 °C for 15 min. Finally, the fluorescent images were observed via CLSM.

All the animal procedures were performed in accordance with a protocol approved by the Committee on the Use of Live Animals in Teaching and Research (CULATR, HKU). Sprague-Dawley rats (eight weeks old) were used to evaluate the in vivo antibacterial property and randomly divided into six groups: Ti without S. aureus and NIR (Ti S. a. (−) NIR(−)), VG@Ti-750 without S. aureus and NIR (VG@Ti-750 S. a. (−) NIR(−)), Ti with S. aureus without NIR (Ti S. a. (+) NIR(−)), VG@Ti-750 with S. aureus without NIR (VG@Ti-750 S. a. (+) NIR(−)), Ti with S. aureus and NIR (Ti S. a. (+) NIR(+)), VG@Ti-750 with S. aureus and NIR (VG@Ti-750 S. a. (+) NIR(+)). Ti and VG@Ti-750 samples were sterilized in 75% ethanol for 2 h and dried at super-clean bench. The rats were anaesthetized with 10% chloral hydrate (360 mg/kg) by intraperitoneal injection. The back skin of each rat was shaved and sterilized. Two defections with the diameter of 10 mm were made on the up and down sides of rat back skin. Then, 10 μL of S. aureus suspension with the density of 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL was introduced to each sample and implanted into rats. The samples with bacteria were exposed to 808 nm laser for 10 min at a power of 1 W/cm2 in NIR group and no laser irradiation was given for the no NIR irradiation group. The rats were euthanized at 1 and 4 days and the tissues and samples were harvested. Bacteria on the samples were investigated by plate counting method. The harvested tissues were evaluated by hematoxylin/eosin (H&E) staining. The primary organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were obtained on 4 days and stained with H&E to assess the in vivo bio-safety of the samples.

All the data are indicated as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistically significant differences were defined by one-way variance and Turkey's multiple comparison tests. A p-value < 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant, and was expressed as symbol “∗”, a p-value < 0.01 was represented by “∗∗”, and a p-value < 0.001 was represented by “∗∗∗”.

Raman spectra of various samples are showed in Fig. 1 a. Raman spectrum of VG@Ti-600 shows a straight line from 1200 to 2800 cm−1, While three distinct peaks of 1351, 1585, and 2706 cm−1 appear on VG@Ti-650, VG@Ti-700, VG@Ti-750, and VG@Ti-800 which correspond to the D, G, and 2D peaks of graphene materials. D peak is attributed to the defect in graphite lattice, G peak is due to the doubly degenerate zone center E
2g mode optical mode vibration and 2D peak is attributed to phonons with opposite momentum in the highest optical branch [46,47]. Based on the results of Raman spectra, it can be concluded that vertical graphene can't be prepared on titanium surface at 600 °C, while it can grow on titanium surface at 650–800 °C. Obvious deformation of titanium on VG@Ti-800 is observed in Fig. S3. Thus, low temperature (600 °C) and high temperature (800 °C) are not suitable for the preparation of vertical graphene on titanium. Therefore, temperatures of 650 °C, and 750 °C were chosen to fabricate vertical graphene films on the titanium surface. XRD patterns of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are presented on Fig. 1b. 2θ at 36.1°, 37.9°, 40.1°, 52.6°, 62.8°, 70.2°, and 75.9° correspond to [100], [002], [101], [102], [110], [103], and [112] crystal planes of α-Ti [48]. Characteristic peak of TiC also be detected which indicates that TiC is formed during the growth process of vertical graphene [49]. 25 points on each sample were analyzed by Raman scattering and the I
D
/I
G rate mappings are revealed in Fig. 1(c and d). The I
D
/I
G ratios of VG@Ti-650 are very similar varying from 0.763 to 0.891, which indicates that the vertical graphene on VG@Ti-650 is uniform. Compared with VG@Ti-650, I
D
/I
G ratios of VG@Ti-750 increase. It suggests that VG@Ti-750 has more defects than VG@Ti-650. The I
2D
/I
G ratio of high-quality (defect free) single-layer graphene is 2. Decrease of I
2D
/I
G ratio suggests that the number of graphene layer is getting thicker [50]. I
2D
/I
G ratios of VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 vary from 0.695 to 0.866 and 0.605–0.641, respectively, which indicates that VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 are multi-layer graphene instead of single layer graphene. The crystallite size along the a-axis (L
a ) of sp2 regions in vertical graphene is determined by the empirical Tuinstra-Koening equation:
L
a = 2.4 × 10−10 × λlaser 4 × I
G
/I
D
which λ laser is the laser excitation wavelength in nm, and I
G and I
D are the Raman intensity of G and D peak, respectively [51]. Crystallite sizes of ordered sp2 regions of VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are 20.33 ± 1.59 nm, and 12.76 ± 0.32 nm, respectively. It suggests that the crystallite size is influenced by the preparation temperature.

Surface morphologies of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are presented in Fig. 1g. Ti displays a ridge-like shape after acid-etch treatment. While sheet-like nanostructures emerge on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750. Graphene on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 is almost perpendicular to the titanium substrate. Surface morphologies of VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 are influenced by initial nucleation stage and the subsequent vertical growth. Kondo et al. reported that graphene sheets were randomly orientated in the initially nucleated stage; however, the growth rate differs between the directions along the expanding graphene sheets and along the stacking. The overshadow effect and the preference in radical diffusion together cause the graphene to grow faster in the vertical direction [52]. The nanoflakes of VG@Ti-750 is smaller compared with VG@Ti-650, while the number of graphene nanoflakes on VG@Ti-750 is much more than that on VG@Ti-650. The preparation temperature influences the nucleation and growth of vertical graphene. High preparation temperature (750 °C) is favorable for the formation of nucleation sites. With the increase of growth temperature, thickness of vertical graphene increases as shown in Fig. 1(h and i). The thicknesses of vertical graphene films on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 are approximately 101.8 nm and 191.3 nm, respectively.

As it is well known, surface wettability can produce an effect on the biological properties. Consequently, water contact angles of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 were measured and the results are displayed in Fig. 2 a. Water contact angles of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are 52.7 ± 1.5°, 106.5 ± 2.7°, and 141.9 ± 4.9°, respectively. Compared with Ti, vertical graphene films on titanium substrate exhibit a more hydrophobic surface due to its hydrophobic property, sheet-like nanostructure.

Zeta potentials were measured from Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 and the results are showed in Fig. 2c. Zeta potentials of various samples increase with the decreasing pH values. At pH = 7.4, zeta potentials of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are −59.3, −49.7, and −45.6 mV, respectively, which indicates that zeta potentials of various samples at pH = 7.4 exhibit the following trend: Ti < VG@Ti-650 < VG@Ti-750.

The UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra obtained from Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are showed in Fig. 2d. There is a fluctuation at the wavelength of 860.8 nm due to the switch between detector and grating. Compared with Ti, VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 possess strong absorption at 808 nm, which can generate heat energy [53]. To investigate the photothermal property, Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 immersed in 1 mL of ultrapure water were separately exposed to the 808 nm laser at a power of 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min. After near-infrared irradiation, surface temperatures of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 increased to 46.1 °C, 51.7 °C, and 57.4 °C, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(e and f). When exposed to air with near-infrared irradiation at a power of 0.5 W/cm2 for 5 min, surface temperatures of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 could reach to 46.3 °C, 53.3 °C, and 63.7 °C, respectively (Fig. 2g). It should be noted that VG@Ti-750 displays markedly photostability under near-infrared irradiation for six cycles as shown in Fig. 2h. Based on this, it can be seen that vertical graphene is an efficient photothermal material.

The antibacterial activities of various samples were assessed using E. coli and S. aureus. As shown in Fig. 3 (a and b), E. coli and S. aureus retain smooth and regular shapes on Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 without NIR, which indicates that vertical graphene on titanium substrate doesn't show antibacterial activity. In NIR group, E. coli and S. aureus on Ti exhibit complete and smooth cell morphology while cell membranes of E. coli and S. aureus on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 have evident wrinkles and distortion. It indicates that vertical graphene on titanium substrate shows good photothermal antibacterial effects.

As shown in Fig. 3(c and d), Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 exhibit no antibacterial activity in control group without NIR. In contrast, after exposing to 808 nm light for 10 min, E. coli and S. aureus on VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are almost dead. Inhibition rates of VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 against E. coli are 87.0% and 93.3%, respectively, and inhibition rates of 95.6% and 99.1% against S. aureus are obtained from VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750, respectively. VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 exhibit excellent sterilization performance which demonstrates the excellent photothermal sterilization performance of vertical graphene.

The live/dead ® baclight™ bacterial viability kits were used to visually observe the bacteria viability. SYTO 9 stain (green fluorescence) generally marks all bacteria. However, propidium iodide (PI) (red fluorescence) penetrates only dead bacteria with compromised membranes [54]. Confocal fluorescence images of E. coli and S. aureus on Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 with and without laser irradiation are displayed in Fig. 3(e-h). No red spots are found on Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 in the control group without NIR, which indicates that bacteria grow well on Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750. A lot of dead bacteria are observed on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 in NIR group. Only few dead bacteria are visualized on Ti in NIR group. It further confirms that vertical graphene on titanium have an effective photothermal antibacterial activity.

Antibacterial mechanisms of GMs have been widely investigated and several predominant mechanisms have been proposed including direct contact of the bacteria with the extremely sharp edges of graphene sheets (nano-knife effect) [55], protein-protein bonding disruption, graphene enters the hydrophobic interface between contacting proteins, leading to their destabilization [56], oxidative stress [57], charge transferring limiting the glycolysis process [58], isolation by wrapping [59]. Besides, many studies have reported that physicochemical properties of GMs containing surface morphology, layer number, lateral size, and surface functionality may have great effect on their antibacterial activities [34]. Therefore, antibacterial mechanisms of GMs are closely related to experimental conditions and designs. For example, Pandit et al. reported that exposed edges of vertical graphene presented antibacterial activity against E. coli and Staphylococcus epidermidis by penetrating the bacterial membranes and draining the cytosolic contents [60]. However, in our present study, vertical graphene itself prepared on titanium surface by PECVD in situ at 650 °C and 750 °C showed no antibacterial performance. This can be attributed to the absence of single-layer graphene sheets with extremely sharp edges as confirmed by the Raman spectroscopy and SEM images. In the present work, photothermal property of vertical graphene were exploited to realize the rapid sterilization under near-infrared light irradiation. Temperature of 63.7 °C can be reached when vertical graphene was exposed to near-infrared irradiation with 0.5 W/cm2 in air for 5 min. By taking advantage of photothermal property, vertical graphene films exhibited excellent antibacterial effects where inhibition rates of 93.3% and 99.1% were obtained against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, when vertical graphene was exposed to the 808 nm laser at a power of 0.8 W/cm2 for 10 min.

MC3T3-E1 cells were applied to assess the biocompatibility of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750. Fig. 4 a displays the cytoskeleton staining of MC3T3-E1 cells after culturing for 1, 4, and 24 h. At the first hour, cells on all the samples show a round morphology. Cells gradually show filopodia and lamellipodia at 4 h, and these filopodia and lamellipodia grew at 24 h. The above results demonstrate that cells adhere and grow well on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750. Results of live/dead cell staining cultured for 4 days on Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 are showed in Fig. 4b. Lots of live cells with green fluorescence can be seen from Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750, while dead cells with red fluorescence be barely observed. It suggests that vertical graphene on titanium surface exhibit no obvious cytotoxicity to osteoblasts cells. To quantitatively analyze the cell proliferation rate, a cell counting kit-8 was used and corresponding result is displayed in Fig. 4c. The results show that, although MC3T3-E1 cells cultured on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 present a lower proliferation rate compared with Ti, the proliferation rates still increase on VG@Ti-650 and VG@Ti-750 as time extends. SEM surface morphologies of MC3T3-E1 cells cultured for 1, 4, and 7 days on various samples are showed in Fig. 4d. MC3T3-E1 cells grow well on Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 where lots of cells can be observed. With the extension of time, cell numbers on various samples increase. After culturing for 7 days, MC3T3-E1 cells almost cover the entire surface of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750.

In vivo antibacterial effects of Ti and VG@Ti-750 was evaluated by implanting samples at the back skin of SD rats (Fig. 5 a). After near-infrared irradiation, surface temperatures of the control group without sample, Ti, and VG@Ti-750 increase to 42.1 °C, 45.3 °C, and 51.9 °C, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5(b and c). As shown in Fig. 5(d and e), Ti and VG@Ti-750 exhibit no antibacterial activity without NIR irradiation at 1 day and 4 days. In contrast, after exposing to 808 nm light for 10 min, S. aureus on VG@Ti-750 are almost dead. Inhibition rates of Ti and VG@Ti-750 against S. aureus are 25.2% and 99.6% at 1 day, respectively, and inhibition rates of 28.7% and 97.7% against S. aureus are obtained from Ti and VG@Ti-750 at 4 days, respectively. VG@Ti-750 exhibits excellent sterilization performance in vivo, which is consistent with the photothermal antibacterial results in vitro. Tissue inflammation caused by S. aureus was investigated by H&E staining (Fig. 5f). At 1 day, a large number of inflammatory cells are observed in Ti S. a. (+) NIR (−) and VG@Ti-750 S. a. (+) NIR (−) groups. There are much fewer inflammatory cells in Ti S. a. (+) NIR (+) group, especially in VG@Ti-750 S. a. (+) NIR (+) group. Fig. S4 shows the H&E staining of main organs containing heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney of the rats after 4 days, and no obvious damage is observed. However, it is worth noting that the biosafety of graphene materials still need to be concerned. Akhavan et al. reported that graphene materials could show some concentration-dependent and long-time side effects on animals and their next generation [61].

In this study, vertical graphene was successfully prepared on titanium surface by PECVD. Vertical graphene on titanium exhibited good corrosion resistance and photothermal property. Temperature of 63.7 °C can be reached when vertical graphene was exposed to near-infrared irradiation with 0.5 W/cm2 in air for 5 min. By taking advantage of photothermal property, vertical graphene films exhibited excellent and rapid antibacterial effects where inhibition rates of 93.3% and 99.1% were observed against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Moreover, vertical graphene on titanium surface exhibit no obvious cytotoxicity to osteoblasts cells. The in vivo results confirmed that vertical graphene with near-infrared light could kill bacteria efficiently and showed no toxicity to the main organs.

Xianming Zhang: Methodology, Experimentation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jiajun Qiu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ji Tan: Writing – review & editing. Dongdong Zhang: Investigation. Ling Wu: Animal experimentation. Yuqin Qiao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Guocheng Wang: Writing – review & editing. Jun Wu: Writing – review & editing. Kelvin W.K. Yeung: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xuanyong Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52001321, 51831011), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (19JC1415500), Shenzhen Science and Technology Research funding (JCYJ20210324120012034), High-end Entrepreneurial and Innovative Teams of Ningbo High-level Talents Project (2018A -09-C).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Luan, T. Huynh, L. Zhao, R. Zhou, Potential Toxicity of Graphene to Cell Functions via Disrupting Protein-Protein Interactions, ACS Nano 9(1) (2015) 663-669.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial activity of graphite, graphite oxide, gtaphene oxide, and reduced graphene oxide: menbrane and oxidative stress

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, T. Zeng, M. Hofmann, E. Burcombe, J. Wei, R. Jiang, Antibacterial Activity of Graphite, Graphite Oxide, Gtaphene Oxide, and Reduced Graphene Oxide: Menbrane and Oxidative Stress, ACS Nano 5(9) (2011) 6971-6980.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Escherichia coli bacteria reduce graphene oxide to bactericidal graphene in a self-limiting manner

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Akhavan, E. Ghaderi, Escherichia coli bacteria reduce graphene oxide to bactericidal graphene in a self-limiting manner, Carbon 50 (2012) 1853-1860.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Wrapping bacteria by graphene nanosheets for isolation from environment, reactivation by sonication, and inactivation by near-infrared irradiation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Akhavan, E. Ghaderi, A. Esfandiar, Wrapping Bacteria by Graphene Nanosheets for Isolation from Environment, Reactivation by Sonication, and Inactivation by Near-Infrared Irradiation, The Journal of Physical Chemistry B 115(19) (2011) 6279-6288.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vertically aligned graphene coating is bactericidal and prevents the formation of bacterial biofilms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Pandit, Z. Cao, V.R.S.S. Mokkapati, E. Celauro, A. Yurgens, M. Lovmar, et al., Vertically Aligned Graphene Coating is Bactericidal and Prevents the Formation of Bacterial Biofilms, Advanced Materials Interfaces 5(7) (2018) 1701331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dose-dependent effects of nanoscale graphene oxide on reproduction capability of mammals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Akhavan, E. Ghaderi, E. Hashemi, E. Akbari, Dose-dependent effects of nanoscale graphene oxide on reproduction capability of mammals, Carbon 95 (2015) 309-317.
